clinical_presentation: Motor weakness or difficulty with movement

differential_diagnoses.justification: Restricted diffusion is highly characteristic of acute ischemia, and multi-focal lesions suggest embolic or systemic causes.
Acute demyelinating lesions can show restricted diffusion, although the absence of contrast enhancement makes active inflammation less likely.
Systemic or CNS vasculitis can result in multi-focal ischemic lesions with restricted diffusion, even without prominent enhancement.

management_plan.immediate_actions: Urgent clinical correlation with patient history and neurological examination.
further_investigations: Comprehensive blood tests (e.g., inflammatory markers, autoimmune screen, thrombophilia screen, infection screen)
Lumbar puncture (if demyelinating or infectious process suspected)
Vascular imaging (e.g., MRA/CTA of brain and neck)
Repeat MRI brain (to assess lesion evolution)
management_plan.follow_up_timeline: Urgent review within days, ideally same-day clinical assessment.

parsed.patient_explanation: Your brain scan shows several small areas that look different from the surrounding brain tissue. These changes suggest there might have been some recent events, possibly like small strokes or a condition affecting the protective covering of your brain cells. There is no sign of a large growth or active swelling. We need to conduct more tests and understand your symptoms to determine the exact cause and the best way to manage it.

triage_priority.justification: The presence of multi-focal acute or subacute brain lesions with restricted diffusion requires urgent clinical assessment and workup to determine the etiology and guide treatment.

red_flags: Multi-focal T2-FLAIR hyperintense lesions with restricted diffusion, indicating acute or subacute brain pathology.
